response: The internet began as a US Department of Defense networking project in the 1960s, with ARPANET first demonstrated in 1969 by sending a message from UCLA to Stanford Research Institute. A major turning point came in 1983 with the adoption of TCP/IP, and in 1989 Tim Berners-Lee created the World Wide Web, introducing HTML, URLs, and HTTP; Mosaic’s 1993 launch then helped bring the web to ordinary users. The main argument is that the internet evolved through key technological and commercial milestones—from the dot-com boom and 2001 crash to social media, smartphones after the iPhone’s 2007 launch, and cloud computing with AWS in 2006—into a transformative global infrastructure. Today, it connects over 5 billion people and supports nearly every part of modern life.
